Second, it would be interesting to understand whether Scarf’s algorithm converges in polynomial time on the bipartite matching polytope for any ordinal matrix \(C\). This would require an understanding of the problem that goes beyond stable marriages.
title: Polynomial-time convergence of Scarf’s algorithm for any ordinal matrix setting
background_latex:
Scarf’s lemma concerns a down-monotone polytope in standard form
\[
P=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^{n+m}_{\ge 0}: Ax=b\},\qquad A=(I\mid A')\in\mathbb{Q}^{n\times(n+m)}_{\ge 0},\ b\in\mathbb{Q}^n_{+},
\]
where \(I\) is the \(n\times n\) identity. A set \(B\subseteq [n+m]\) of \(n\) column indices is an \((A,b)\)-basis if the corresponding submatrix \(A_B\) is nonsingular and the associated basic solution is feasible for \(Ax=b,\ x\ge 0\). An \(n\times(n+m)\) integer matrix \(C=(c_{i,j})\) is an ordinal matrix if, in each row \(i\), the entries are distinct and satisfy
\[
c_{i,i}<c_{i,k}<c_{i,j}\quad \text{for all } i\neq j\in[n],\ k\in [n+m]\setminus [n].
\]
Given a set \(D\subseteq [n+m]\) with \(|D|=n\), define its utility vector \(u\in\mathbb{Q}^n\) by
\[
u_i=\min_{j\in D} c_{i,j}\quad (i\in[n]).
\]
The set \(D\) is an ordinal basis if for every column \(h\in[n+m]\) there exists some row \(i\in[n]\) such that \(u_i\ge c_{i,h}\). A dominating basis for \((A,b,C)\) is a set \(B\) that is simultaneously an \((A,b)\)-basis and an ordinal basis; the corresponding vertex of \(P\) is a dominating vertex.

Scarf’s algorithm is a pivoting procedure that maintains a feasible basis \(B\) for \((A,b)\) and an ordinal basis \(D\) for \(C\) with \(|B\cap D|\ge n-1\), performing:
1) a cardinal pivot (basis exchange in \(A\)) that inserts the unique element of \(D\setminus B\) into \(B\), and
2) an ordinal pivot (basis exchange in \(C\)) that replaces an element of \(D\cap B\) by a uniquely determined new column to keep \(D\) an ordinal basis.
On nondegenerate polytopes, Scarf proved termination; for degenerate polytopes, termination depends on how ties are broken in the cardinal pivot.

In this paper’s main application, \(P\) is the (degenerate) bipartite matching polytope of a bipartite graph (stable marriage setting), with \(A\) the node-edge incidence matrix augmented with slack variables (loops) and \(b\) the all-ones vector. The paper proves polynomial-time convergence for one specific ordinal matrix \(C^*\) constructed from the preference lists (and more generally for certain consistent \(C\)).
problem_definition_latex:
Let \(P=\{x\in\mathbb{R}^{n+m}_{\ge 0}: Ax=b\}\) be the bipartite matching polytope in standard form \(A=(I\mid A')\) with \(b\) the all-ones vector, and let \(C\in\mathbb{Z}^{n\times(n+m)}\) be an arbitrary ordinal matrix.

Determine whether Scarf’s algorithm converges in polynomial time on input \((A,b,C)\) for every ordinal matrix \(C\), and if so, prove a polynomial upper bound on the number of pivot iterations (or provide a counterexample showing that polynomial-time convergence fails for some ordinal matrix \(C\)).

location_in_paper: Section 8 (Conclusions and Future Work), page 35 (arXiv version dated 1 Mar 2023).
open_problem_quote: Second, it would be interesting to understand whether Scarf’s algorithm converges in polynomial time on the bipartite matching polytope for any ordinal matrix \(C\). This would require an understanding of the problem that goes beyond stable marriages.
subject_classification: Computational complexity and pivoting algorithms in combinatorial optimization
counterexample_suitability_rationale: One can algorithmically generate bipartite graphs and ordinal matrices and simulate Scarf’s algorithm, but the behavior depends on cardinal pivot tie-breaking under degeneracy, so a counterexample search must also range over pivot rules or specify a canonical rule. Efficient verification of superpolynomial paths is nontrivial but empirical searches for long pivot paths are feasible on moderate sizes.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Evaluating a candidate proof strategy or pivot rule for all ordinal matrices requires symbolic reasoning and worst-case analysis rather than a straightforward computable fitness function. While small-instance simulation could provide feedback, it is not a reliable proxy for polynomial-time guarantees on all inputs.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
